comm.title: Digital Communication System Simulation with Noise and BER Analysis
comm.context: Personal project developed to model and analyze a complete baseband digital communication system under noisy channel conditions, reinforcing concepts from Mobile Communications, Radio Receivers, and Signal Transmission Theory. The project included end-to-end processing from message encoding to reception and decoding.
comm.problem:
Evaluate the performance of different digital modulation schemes (BPSK, QPSK/PSK, and QAM) under an Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN) channel and analyze their impact on Bit Error Rate (BER).
The system was designed to implement the full transmission chain:
- Conversion of a human-readable message (ASCII) into binary
- Bitstream grouping and symbol mapping
- Digital modulation
- Transmission through an AWGN channel
- Demodulation and decoding
- BER computation and message reconstruction
comm.contribution:
• Designed and implemented a complete transmitter–receiver chain
  • Developed ASCII-to-bit encoding and bitstream management
  • Implemented symbol mapping and demapping for multiple modulation schemes
  • Modeled an AWGN channel
  • Computed BER vs SNR performance curves
  • Compared spectral efficiency vs robustness trade-offs
comm.tools: MATLAB Digital modulation theory (BPSK, PSK, QAM) AWGN channel modeling Statistical BER estimation
comm.results: Generated BER vs SNR curves for multiple modulation schemes. Verified expected theoretical trends (higher-order QAM more spectrally efficient but less noise-robust). Successfully reconstructed transmitted messages under different SNR conditions. Identified potential system improvements such as adding error-detection mechanisms (e.g., parity check or CRC).
comm.learned: Strengthened practical intuition on modulation trade-offs, spectral efficiency, and noise sensitivity. Gained hands-on experience in implementing a complete digital communication chain and evaluating system-level performance under stochastic channel conditions.

diel.title: Design and Optimization of Dielectric Waveguide Structures and Transitions in PCB
diel.data: Bachelor's Thesis · Supervised by Mario Faliero · 2025 · RF Systems
diel.context: Bachelor's thesis developed in collaboration with a doctoral research team, focused on the electromagnetic design, fabrication and validation of dielectric transmission lines and transitions compatible with PCB manufacturing. The project included full design ownership and formal thesis defense.
diel.problem:
Design, simulate, and fabricate low-loss dielectric waveguide structures suitable for PCB integration, ensuring:
- Minimal insertion loss
- Good impedance matching
- Efficient and smooth transitions between guiding structures
- Stable performance in the K and Ka bands (21–30 GHz)
A key challenge was achieving robust transitions between dielectric guides and conventional waveguide structures while maintaining mechanical stability and low return loss at high frequencies.
diel.contribution:
• Full-wave EM modeling and simulation
  • Parametric sweeps and geometry optimization
  • Design of 3 dielectric guiding structures
  • Design and tuning of 6 transitions between structures
  • Analysis of S-parameters and field distributions
  • PCB layout preparation for fabrication
  • Physical manufacturing and experimental validation
diel.tools: CST Studio Suite MATLAB VNA (Vector Network Analyser) PCB layout design tools
diel.results: Achieved low insertion loss and acceptable return loss within target frequency K/Ka band (21-30GHz). Demonstrated manufacturable PCB-compatible dielectric guiding solution. Validated transition performance with consistent impedance matching. Identified the need for a more mechanically robust dielectric-to-waveguide transition to further reduce return loss and improve structural stability.
diel.learned: Developed strong expertise in high-frequency dielectric propagation, transition engineering and simulation-driven optimization under fabrication constraints. Gained a broader system-level perspective by considering mechanical robustness, manufacturability, and transmission efficiency for real-world applications such as mobile communications and satellite systems.

dosis.title: Patch Antenna Array (DoSis)
dosis.data: Student Job in Research (KIT) · Team of 5 PhD · 2025 · Antenna Systems in Radar + Integrated Circuit Research Environment
dosis.context: Research-oriented multidisciplinary project focused on the design and optimization of a compact patch antenna array with symmetrical input and output ports for radar applications. The work was conducted in collaboration with a PhD research team developing a Doppler-based target auto-processing chip.
dosis.problem:
Design a microstrip antenna array under strict physical constraints (fixed PCB height, substrate materials, and predefined chip port widths) capable of:
- Radiationg at 45° relative to the radar reference axis
- Achieving a minimum gain of 7 dBi
- Maintaining inter-port coupling below −30 dB
Since the chip port dimensions were fixed, the primary challenge was to ensure proper 50 Ω impedance matching for maximum power transfer while minimizing mutual coupling between input and output ports.
dosis.contribution:
• Full EM modeling and simulation of the antenna array
  • Parametric sweeps and geometry optimization
  • Design and evaluation of decoupling structures
  • Analysis of radiation pattern, impedance matching, gain, bandwidth and coupling performance
  • Iterative refinement of the feed network and array configuration
dosis.tools: CST Studio Suite (full-wave FEM simulation) Microstrip line impedance analytical calculator Book: Antenna Theory - Balanis Book: Microwave Engineering - Pozar
dosis.results: Achieved 11.5 dBi gain (exceeding the 7 dBi target). Reduced S21 (inter-port coupling) to -20dB (10dB improvement still required to meet specification). Improved impedance matching through a double-layer PCB implementation. Validated radiation characteristics and field distribution through full-wave simulation.
dosis.learned: Developed a deeper understanding of microstrip-based array behavior, feed network design, and coupling mitigation techniques. Strengthened practical expertise in CST-driven optimization and high-frequency PCB-constrained antenna design.

sar.title: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) Target Simulation and Reconstruction
sar.data: Personal Project · Individual · 2025 · Spaceborne Radar Remote Systems
sar.context: Personal MATLAB-based simulation project developed to deepen theoretical understanding from the courses Radar Systems Engineering and Spaceborne Radar Remote Sensing. The project focused on modeling SAR signal generation and target reconstruction from first principles.
sar.problem:
Simulate the radar return from a point target using chirp waveforms and reconstruct the target image through matched filtering and convolution-based processing. The primary challenges included:
- Accurately modeling the point target response as a function of range
- Incorporating radar platform motion and its Doppler frequency effects
- Accounting for received power variation with azimuth angle
- Achieving proper range and azimuth compression
sar.contribution:
• Modeled transmitted linear FM chirp waveform
  • Simulated target reflection considering propagation delay and motion
  • Implemented convolution-based matched filtering
  • Performed range compression
  • Implemented azimuth processing and analyzed resolution limitations
  • Evaluated resolution trade-offs between range and azimuth domains
sar.tools: MATLAB Linear FM signal modeling Matched filtering Range and azimuth compression algorithms SAR
sar.results: Successful range compression using matched filtering.  Partial azimuth reconstruction achieved. Identified resolution degradation due to motion modeling and Doppler approximation constraints. Evaluated sensitivity of azimuth resolution to platform velocity assumptions
sar.learned: Developed a stronger understanding of SAR signal processing fundamentals, particularly the role of Doppler processing and motion modeling in azimuth resolution. Gained practical insight into the sensitivity of SAR reconstruction to modeling assumptions.

vivaldi.title: Antenna Design: Vivaldi, Circular Patch, Can Antenna
vivaldi.data: Workshop · Individual · 2024 · Antenna Simulation and Deisgn
vivaldi.context: Course workshop project within the Antennas and Antenna Arrays subject, focused on the design, simulation, fabrication, and measurement of broadband and microstrip antennas operating at 2.4 GHz. The project included parametrization and optimization of Vivaldi, circular patch, and cylindrical waveguide (“can”) antennas using CST Studio Suite, followed by array simulation in MATLAB and experimental validation through real-world manufacturing and measurements.
vivaldi.problem:
Design antennas operating in their dominant mode with stable radiation patterns and efficient performance in the 2.4 GHz ISM band, ensuring proper 50 Ω impedance matching.
The primary challenge was selecting and optimizing multiple geometric parameters (e.g., patch radius, waveguide diameter, taper length, feed position) to maximize gain and bandwidth while maintaining impedance matching and radiation stability.
vivaldi.contribution:
• Geometry Parametrization using parameter sweeps
  • Optimization of antenna dimensions for resonance and gain
  • Beamwidth and field distribution analysis for single elements and arrays
  • Simulation of antenna arrays in MATLAB
  • Fabrication of selected designs
  • Measurement and validation in an anechoic chamber
  • Comparison of simulated vs. measured S-parameters and radiation patterns
vivaldi.tools: CST Studio Suite MATLAB Analytical antenna modeling Vector Network Analyzer (VNA) Anechoic chamber measurements
vivaldi.results: Achieved resonance at 2.4 GHz in both simulation and fabricated prototypes.  Acceptable return loss and radiation efficiency. Broadband impedance matching (Vivaldi design). Measured radiation patterns, gain, and S-parameters with good agreement between simulation and experimental results.
vivaldi.learned: Developed practical understanding of broadband and resonant antenna behavior, including the impact of geometry on bandwidth, gain, and matching. Strengthened knowledge of dominant modes and cavity-based radiation mechanisms, and gained hands-on experience in fabrication quality and its influence on return loss and measurement accuracy.
